cards.title: Acupuncture Therapeutics — Principles & Mechanisms
cards._legacyHtml:
Acupuncture in BNYS Practice
Acupuncture involves the insertion of fine sterile needles into specific anatomical points (acupoints) along meridian channels to restore the flow of Qi (vital energy), correct energetic imbalances, and promote self-healing. Modern neuroscience has elucidated multiple mechanisms: endorphin release, descending pain inhibition, autonomic modulation, and connective tissue mechanotransduction. Evidence base is strong for pain conditions, nausea/vomiting, and certain functional disorders.
MechanismEvidenceClinical Significance
Endorphin / Enkephalin Release★★★ Well establishedLow-frequency electroacupuncture (2Hz) → β-endorphin, enkephalin. High-frequency (100Hz) → dynorphin. Naloxone (opioid antagonist) partially blocks acupuncture analgesia
Gate Control Pain Modulation★★★ Well establishedNeedle stimulation activates Aβ fibres → closes pain gate at dorsal horn → inhibits Aδ and C fibre transmission
Descending Inhibitory Control (DNIC)★★ Good evidenceAcupuncture activates periaqueductal grey (PAG) → releases serotonin and noradrenaline into dorsal horn → widespread pain inhibition
Autonomic Modulation★★ Good evidenceST36 acupuncture → vagus nerve activation → reduced sympathetic tone, increased HRV. Useful in hypertension, functional GI disorders, stress
Connective Tissue Mechanotransduction★★ EmergingNeedle rotation wraps fascial collagen → mechanical signal propagates along fascial planes (explains sensation along meridian pathways)
Anti-inflammatory Effects★★ ModerateSP6, ST36 stimulation → ↓ IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6. Cholinergic anti-inflammatory pathway via vagus nerve

cards.title: Key Acupoints — Clinical Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
The 14 Meridians & Major Points
Traditional Chinese medicine describes 14 main meridians with approximately 361 classical acupoints. The most commonly used points in evidence-based acupuncture practice are listed below with their primary indications.
PointLocationPrimary IndicationKey Clinical Use
ST36 Zusanli4 fingerbreadths below patella, 1 fingerbreadth lateral to tibial crestImmune support, GI disorders, fatigue, energy★ Master tonification point — most studied point globally. Chemotherapy-induced nausea, IBD, immune modulation, depression
LI4 HeguDorsum of hand, midpoint of 2nd metacarpal, radial sidePain (face, head, neck), labour analgesia, immune★ Primary analgesic point. Headache, dental pain, facial pain. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (stimulates uterine contractions)
SP6 Sanyinjiao3 cun above medial malleolus, posterior to tibial borderGynaecological, urinary, digestive, insomniaIntersection of Spleen, Kidney, Liver meridians — powerful point. Dysmenorrhoea, PCOS, insomnia, oedema. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy
PC6 Neiguan2 cun above wrist crease, between palmaris longus and FCR tendonsNausea, vomiting, anxiety, cardiac palpitations★ Grade A evidence for chemotherapy-induced nausea. Motion sickness (Sea-Bands). Available as acupressure — effective without needles
GV20 BaihuiCrown of head — midpoint between apex of earsHeadache, cognitive function, depression, prolapseGoverning vessel — raises Yang energy. Used in depression, cognitive decline, postural hypotension, uterine prolapse (raising Qi)
KD1 YongquanPlantar surface, anterior ⅓ of foot (between 2nd & 3rd MT heads)Hypertension, insomnia, grounding, anxietyKidney meridian source — descends excess Yang. Used as acupressure for hypertension, insomnia, severe anxiety (emergency calming)
HT7 ShenmenUlnar end of wrist crease, radial side of flexor carpi ulnarisAnxiety, insomnia, palpitations, emotional disordersHeart meridian — calms Shen (spirit). Anxiety, insomnia, OCD, post-traumatic stress. Use with SP6 for insomnia combination
BL40 WeizhongMidpoint of popliteal creaseAcute low back pain, leg pain, skin disordersCommand point for back. Acute LBP — often immediate relief. Combine with BL60 for bladder meridian back pain
GB21 JianjingMidpoint between C7 spinous process and acromionNeck/shoulder pain, headache, breast disordersTension headache from neck — combine with GB20. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (strong descending action)
LV3 TaichongDorsum of foot, between 1st and 2nd metatarsal, proximal junctionLiver Qi stagnation, stress, menstrual disorders, hypertensionLiver source point. Stress, anger, PMS, hypertension, eye disorders. LI4 + LV3 = "Four Gates" — powerful full-body Qi regulation

cards.title: Evidence-Based Acupuncture Protocols by Condition
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionEvidence GradePrimary PointsProtocol
Chronic Low Back Pain★★★ Grade A (Cochrane)BL23, BL40, GV4, KD3, ashi points10 sessions, twice weekly × 5 weeks. Electroacupuncture (2Hz) superior to manual for chronic pain. Add BL60 for radiating pain
Neck Pain (non-specific)★★★ Grade AGB21, GB20, BL11, SI3, LI48–10 sessions weekly. Dry needling of upper trapezius trigger points effective adjunct
Chemotherapy-induced Nausea★★★ Grade A (SIO endorsed)PC6 (bilateral), ST36Begin 1 day before chemo, continue 3 days post. Acupressure bands (Sea-Bands) on PC6 as home adjunct
Headache / Migraine★★★ Grade AGB20, GV20, LI4, LV3, ST44 (migraine)12 sessions over 3 months reduces frequency equal to prophylactic medication (topiramate). No adverse effects
Osteoarthritis of Knee★★★ Grade AST35, EX-LE5, SP10, ST34, BL4010–15 sessions. Pain reduction 40%, function improvement 30% vs sham. Add electroacupuncture for superior analgesia
Dysmenorrhoea★★★ Grade ASP6, SP8, CV4, CV6, LV3Begin 1 week before menstruation. Continue during menstruation (days 1–3). 3 cycles treatment for lasting effect
Insomnia★★ Grade BHT7, SP6, KD3, Anmian (extra point), GV208–10 sessions. Ear acupuncture (Shenmen, Heart, Kidney points) effective adjunct
Menopausal Hot Flushes★★★ Grade ASP6, KD3, KD7, CV4, LV3, BL2310 sessions over 6 weeks — reduces frequency and severity. Comparable to SSRI effect size
Anxiety / PTSD★★ Grade BHT7, PC6, GV20, LV3, EX-HN3 (Yintang)8–12 sessions. PTSD: comparable to CBT in military veteran RCTs. Combines well with mindfulness
Allergic Rhinitis★★ Grade BLI4, LI20, BL12, ST36, GV23Weekly × 8 sessions. Reduces symptom scores and rescue antihistamine use

cards.title: Needle Technique & Safety — Clinical Standards
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Needle Selection Gauge: 0.16–0.30mm diameter. Length: 13–25mm (shallow/facial points), 25–40mm (limbs, back), 40–75mm (gluteal, deep points). Always use single-use sterile pre-packaged needles (EN ISO 17664 compliant). Never reuse. Dispose in sharps container immediately after use. Tube insertion technique (guide tube) is safer for beginners.
2
De Qi Sensation The characteristic therapeutic sensation: heaviness, distension, aching, warmth, tingling, or mild electric sensation at needle site or along meridian. NOT sharp pain or burning. De Qi indicates nerve activation and is associated with better outcomes. If patient reports sharp lancinating pain → needle tip on periosteum or nerve — withdraw 1–2mm and redirect.
3
Needle Retention Time Standard: 20–30 minutes. Children/elderly/frail: 10–15 minutes. Acute conditions: shorter. Chronic conditions: longer (30 min). During retention: patient must not move significantly — prevent bent needles. Check patient every 5–10 min. Electroacupuncture adds continuous electrical stimulation via wires clipped to needle handles.
4
Post-Needling Care Count all needles in and out — document count. Press gently with dry cotton ball on removal. Advise patient: avoid strenuous exercise for 2hrs post-treatment, rest if tired (common — beneficial), avoid alcohol, report any unusual symptoms. Common post-treatment effects: local bruising (benign), temporary symptom aggravation in first 1–2 sessions (healing response), drowsiness.
Adverse EventFrequencyManagement
Minor bleeding / bruisingCommon (~3%)Apply firm pressure 1–2 min. Reassure patient. Document.
Vasovagal syncopeUncommon (~0.5%)Supine position, remove needles, leg elevation, observe. Prevention: never needle fasting, fearful, or fatigued patients in upright position
Bent or stuck needleRareDo not force removal — rotate gently in opposite direction while asking patient to relax. Never leave bent needle in situ.
PneumothoraxVery rare (<0.01%)Risk at BL13, BL14, BL15, GB21 — never angle needles toward pleural space. If suspected: emergency referral immediately
Infection (if sterile technique breached)Very rareAlways use single-use sterile needles. Skin prep with 70% isopropyl alcohol. Never needle through infected or broken skin.
Absolute Contraindications to Acupuncture
Bleeding disorders / therapeutic anticoagulation (relative — use with caution). Pacemaker if electroacupuncture used. Pregnancy: avoid LI4, SP6, GB21, BL60, BL67 — strong emmenagogue/descending points. Malignancy at needle site. Active skin infection at proposed site. Immunocompromised (severe — risk of infection). Needle phobia (use acupressure instead).
